subject: ap-comparative-government
theme: How citizens think, participate, and organize is shaped by political culture and regime type — and the deep social cleavages running through every course country constantly challenge governments to balance order and individual liberty.

core_concepts.title: Civil Society and Regime Type
core_concepts.detail: Civil society comprises voluntary associations — NGOs, trade unions, religious organizations, independent media, professional groups — that operate autonomously from the state. A robust civil society serves as an agent of democratization: it monitors government, exposes malfeasance, builds citizens' organizational experience, and represents minority interests. Regime type determines how free civil society is. The UK has strong, independent civil society. Russia severely restricts it through foreign-agent laws, registration requirements, and harassment of NGOs. China suppresses independent civil society via registration monitoring and shutdown powers. Iran licenses and controls civil society organizations and revokes media licenses when publications are deemed anti-religious. Nigeria and Mexico have developing civil societies — MOSOP in Nigeria's Niger Delta is a notable CED example of civil society advocating for an ethnic minority group's rights.

core_concepts.title: Political Culture and Socialization
core_concepts.detail: Political culture is the shared set of attitudes, values, and beliefs citizens hold about how their political system works and should work. It is shaped by geography, historical experience, and religious tradition. Iran's political culture is deeply influenced by Shia Islamic theology, which legitimizes clerical governance. Russia's post-Soviet political culture carries inherited low institutional trust and tolerance for strong-man leadership. Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals acquire their political beliefs — from family, schools, peers, media, and religious institutions. The key comparative distinction: authoritarian regimes apply deliberate, concerted state-directed socialization (schools and media controlled to reinforce conformity), while democratic regimes allow pluralist socialization from competing sources. China and Russia are the CED's primary examples of state-directed socialization.

core_concepts.title: Political Ideologies
core_concepts.detail: The CED requires students to distinguish six political ideologies. Individualism: belief that personal civil liberties and freedoms should take priority over government restrictions — associated with liberal democracies. Neoliberalism: belief in free markets, privatization, deregulation, and minimal government intervention in the economy — the policy dominant in Mexico's 1990s reforms and in the UK. Communism: belief in abolition of private property and near-total government control of the economy — China's official ideology. Socialism: belief in reducing income disparities and nationalizing major industries — distinct from communism in degree. Fascism: extreme nationalist ideology favoring authoritarian rule and ethnic majority dominance — not a current course-country regime type, but testable as an ideological category. Populism: framing politics as a conflict between ordinary people and corrupt elites — associated with Mexico (Zapatista movement, AMLO's movement historically) and Russia (Putin's nationalist populism).

core_concepts.title: Rule of Law vs. Rule by Law; Post-Materialism
core_concepts.detail: Rule of law: the government itself is bound by the same laws as citizens; all actors are equally subject to established, impartial legal rules — characteristic of the UK and (in transition) Mexico and Nigeria. Rule by law: the state uses law as an instrument of control over citizens while remaining above it — characteristic of China under the CPC and Russia's judicial system, which targets opponents rather than constraining state power. This distinction is one of the most frequently tested pairs in the course. Post-materialism: as societies become wealthier and citizens' material needs are secured, values shift from economic security toward self-expression, environmental protection, and quality-of-life concerns. The UK is the course example of post-materialist values — Brexit debates, environmental activism, and social equality demands reflect this shift. Post-materialism is absent from authoritarian course countries where political expression remains constrained.

core_concepts.title: Political Participation: Types and Regime Differences
core_concepts.detail: Political participation can be voluntary (citizens freely choose to act) or coerced (the state pressures or requires action). It can be formal (voting, running for office, contacting officials) or informal (protests, social media campaigns, petitions). It ranges from regime-supportive to oppositional behavior. Authoritarian regimes encourage participation to manufacture legitimacy — elections serve as a safety valve (channeling discontent into a controlled outlet) or create an illusion of influence (the outcome is predetermined but citizens appear to have input). Democratic regimes use elections to allow genuine citizen control of policy making. Russia illustrates competitive authoritarianism: elections are contested but the result is effectively managed through media control, party registration barriers, and selective law enforcement. The UK used referenda for Brexit (2016), Scottish independence (2014), and Welsh/Northern Irish devolution — direct citizen votes on major policy questions.

core_concepts.title: Social Cleavages by Country
core_concepts.detail: Social cleavages are deep, persistent divisions within a society along ethnic, religious, class, or regional lines that shape political conflict. Each course country has distinct cleavages. China: ethnic divide between Han majority (over 90%) and recognized minorities — especially Uighurs (Muslim Turkic minority in Xinjiang) and Tibetans (Buddhist minority in southwest) — both with separatist pressures. Iran: Shi'a-Sunni religious divide, ethnic divisions between Persian majority and Azerbaijani/Kurdish minorities; Kurdish separatism is a persistent pressure. Mexico: ethnic divisions between Amerindian (indigenous) population and mestizos/whites; regional north-south economic divide; the Zapatista movement (1994 Chiapas uprising) is the CED's primary cleavage-driven autonomy demand example. Nigeria: overlapping ethnic (Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, Igbo — 250+ total groups), religious (Muslim-majority north vs. Christian/animist south), and regional cleavages; Boko Haram emerged from the unmanaged north-south religious cleavage. Russia: ethnic Russians vs. Muslim-minority Chechens in the Caucasus — Chechen separatism (two wars: 1994–96, 1999–2009) is the primary Russian cleavage example. UK: Scottish-English-Welsh-Irish regional/national cleavage; Northern Ireland Protestant-Catholic divide; the Scottish independence movement is an ongoing autonomy demand.

core_concepts.title: Media, Civil Liberties, and State Repression
core_concepts.detail: Civil liberties (freedoms from government interference) and civil rights (equal treatment under law) vary sharply by regime type. The CED identifies three media restriction mechanisms: China's Great Firewall (internet censorship system blocking political criticism and foreign websites); Iran's court system that revokes press licenses when publications are found to carry anti-religious or nationally harmful content; and Russia's nationalization of most broadcast media, enabling tight control over political news and opposition coverage. Governmental transparency — the degree to which governments make decision-making accessible — is high in democratic regimes and low in authoritarian ones. State repression (using force or law to suppress opposition) is most common in authoritarian regimes: the 2009 Iranian Green Movement (mass protests following disputed presidential election results, alleging Ahmadinejad's re-election was fraudulent) was suppressed by security forces — a key CED example of authoritarian suppression of oppositional participation.

exam_tip: Unit 3 produces high-frequency exam questions on two concepts: first, the rule of law vs. rule by law distinction — students must be able to apply these terms accurately to specific course countries, not just define them abstractly; second, social cleavages by country — the exam will give you a description of a cleavage and ask which country it describes, so you must know each country's specific cleavage types (Nigeria's overlapping ethnic + religious + regional cleavages are the most complex and most tested).
